{{TOC right}}

== Section 2 — Authorized RMMs ==

This is the canonical reference for what RMMs are sanctioned in the environment. If an analyst sees an RMM artifact and cannot find a matching row below, treat the activity as unauthorized until proven otherwise. This table is the single source of truth that downstream triage logic depends on; keep it current.

'''Maintenance:''' Owner = TH Lead. Review cadence: quarterly, plus on any change request that introduces or retires an RMM. Tenant/Instance ID changes (e.g., ScreenConnect on-prem rebuild) require same-day update.

{| class="wikitable" style="width:100%;"
|+ Authorized RMMs in <ENVIRONMENT>
! style="background:#1F4E79; color:#FFFFFF;" | RMM
! style="background:#1F4E79; color:#FFFFFF;" | Owner Team
! style="background:#1F4E79; color:#FFFFFF;" | Approved Use Case
! style="background:#1F4E79; color:#FFFFFF;" | Tenant / Instance ID
! style="background:#1F4E79; color:#FFFFFF;" | Approved Host Classes
! style="background:#1F4E79; color:#FFFFFF;" | Approved Installer (Account / Method)
|-
| ScreenConnect || EUC / Service Desk || End-user remote support; on-demand technician sessions || <code><INSTANCE_HASH></code> (single instance) || Workstations; non-clinical servers || SCCM as SYSTEM, or <code>svc-sccm-deploy</code>
|-
| <RMM> || <Team> || <Use case> || <Tenant ID> || <Host classes> || <Account / Method>
|-
| <RMM> || <Team> || <Use case> || <Tenant ID> || <Host classes> || <Account / Method>
|-
| <RMM> || <Team> || <Use case> || <Tenant ID> || <Host classes> || <Account / Method>
|}

=== How to populate this table ===

* '''RMM''' — vendor product name. One row per RMM family (do not split by version).
* '''Owner Team''' — team accountable for the deployment. They are the escalation point when an analyst needs a yes/no on a specific install.
* '''Approved Use Case''' — narrow this. "General IT support" is too vague to be actionable; "end-user remote support; on-demand technician sessions" lets an analyst recognize when an install falls outside scope.
* '''Tenant/Instance ID''' — the identifier that distinguishes our tenant from any other. ScreenConnect: instance hash. Atera: AccountId GUID. Action1: OrganizationId GUID. AnyDesk: agent ID(s). [[#Section 4 — Per-RMM Profiles|Per-RMM Profiles]] section gives the extraction logic for each.
* '''Approved Host Classes''' — workstation, server (with subtype), specific user populations. "All hosts" is rarely correct; most authorized RMMs deploy to a defined subset.
* '''Approved Installer (Account / Method)''' — the SubjectUserName in 4697 that should appear when this RMM is installed legitimately, and the deployment method (SCCM, Intune, manual by approved admin). Anything else in 4697 SubjectUserName = Tier 1 hit per [[#Section 3 — Disposition Triage|Section 3]].

'''Note on "shadow IT":''' Unauthorized but legitimate use (a team running an unsanctioned RMM for a real business purpose) and unauthorized malicious use produce identical Section 2 outcomes initially. Disambiguation happens in Section 3 triage. Do not pre-disposition based on assumed intent.

== Section 3 — Disposition Triage ==

Triage logic for an RMM artifact (4697 service install, 4688 process execution, MDE DeviceProcessEvents/DeviceRegistryEvents/DeviceFileEvents). Indicators are stacked. Tier 1 indicators are individually high-confidence and a single hit warrants escalation. Tier 2 indicators require two or more to combine before escalation. Tier 3 indicators are behavioral and confirmatory — they upgrade existing Tier 1/2 dispositions to confirmed compromise.

'''Decision rule:''' 1× Tier 1 OR 2× Tier 2 → escalate to investigation. Any Tier 3 hit on top of a Tier 1/2 disposition → upgrade to confirmed compromise and engage IR.

{| class="wikitable" style="width:100%;"
! style="background:#1F4E79; color:#FFFFFF; width:8%;" | Tier
! style="background:#1F4E79; color:#FFFFFF; width:55%;" | Indicator
! style="background:#1F4E79; color:#FFFFFF; width:37%;" | Disposition Logic
|- style="background:#F8CBAD;"
| '''Tier 1''' || RMM is not on the [[#Section 2 — Authorized RMMs|Authorized RMMs]] list || Single hit. Treat as intrusion artifact until shadow-IT confirmed. Atera and Action1 specifically: ~90% of unauthorized hits in current intrusion data are malicious.
|- style="background:#F8CBAD;"
|  || RMM binary executes or installs from a user-writable path (Temp, AppData, Public, Downloads, ProgramData\<random-guid>) || Single hit. Vendor MSIs do not install here. Operator behavior.
|- style="background:#F8CBAD;"
|  || Service installed by a non-SYSTEM, non-IT-admin account || Single hit. 4697 SubjectUserName ≠ expected. Standard user installing remote-access service is a kill-chain artifact.
|- style="background:#F8CBAD;"
|  || Two or more distinct RMMs on the same endpoint || Single hit (except IT admin workstations). Operators routinely deploy a second RMM as redundant persistence.
|- style="background:#F8CBAD;"
|  || Authorized RMM family present, but tenant/instance ID does not match ours || Single hit. A second ScreenConnect tenant or different Atera customer ID = different operator. Use the per-RMM profile for the extraction logic.
|- style="background:#F8CBAD;"
|  || Binary is unsigned, or signer ≠ vendor norm || Single hit. Tampered build or attacker re-pack. Cross-check DeviceFileCertificateInfo.
|- style="background:#FFE699;"
| '''Tier 2''' || Suspicious parent process for the install (powershell, mshta, wscript, cscript, rundll32, regsvr32, Office, browser, certutil, bitsadmin) || Two-hit threshold. Pivot to 4688 within ±5 min of 4697 to identify parent.
|- style="background:#FFE699;"
|  || Install timing off-hours, no change ticket, no preceding deployment activity (SCCM/Intune push, ticketed remote session) || Two-hit threshold. Cross-reference change-management.
|- style="background:#FFE699;"
|  || Install path inside Program Files but not the vendor's known directory (e.g., AnyDesk.exe in Common Files, Intel, System32) || Two-hit threshold. Vendors do not pick weird Program Files subdirectories.
|- style="background:#FFE699;"
|  || Single-host or near-single-host deployment of a managed RMM || Two-hit threshold. Stack 4697 by Service_Name across the estate; bottom of the stack-rank is suspect.
|- style="background:#FFE699;"
|  || Service install with auto-start type and SYSTEM run, but no entry in software inventory || Two-hit threshold. The CMDB / Intune / Tanium gap is the signal.
|- style="background:#C6E0B4;"
| '''Tier 3''' || Outbound connection from RMM agent to a non-corporate tenant (different ScreenConnect instance, different Atera customer subdomain, different AnyDesk relay) || Confirmatory. Per-RMM profile defines the tenant-identifier extraction.
|- style="background:#C6E0B4;"
|  || RMM agent listening on a port for incoming connections (vs. pure outbound) || Confirmatory. AnyDesk:7070, MeshAgent/RURat configurable. Most modern RMMs are outbound-only to SaaS broker; listener mode is unusual.
|- style="background:#C6E0B4;"
|  || RMM agent spawning interactive shells (cmd.exe, powershell.exe, wscript, mshta) or LOLBINs || Confirmatory of hands-on-keyboard. Legitimate technician sessions do this rarely and with a paired ticket.
|- style="background:#C6E0B4;"
|  || Discovery / lateral movement commands within 60 minutes of install (whoami /all, net group, nltest, quser, AD enumeration, SMB scanning) || Confirmatory. Standard post-deployment recon footprint.
|- style="background:#C6E0B4;"
|  || RMM agent dropping files outside its own install path (Temp, AppData, network shares) || Confirmatory. Agent should mostly do RPC/network I/O, not file staging.
|}

=== Triage workflow (analyst sequence on a 4697 hit) ===

# '''Look up the RMM in [[#Section 2 — Authorized RMMs|Section 2]].''' Not on the list → Tier 1 hit. Stop and escalate. (Other indicators still gathered for the case, but disposition is already set.)
# '''Check install path.''' Compare 4697 Service_File_Name against the per-RMM profile's expected paths. User-writable path → Tier 1 hit.
# '''Check installer identity.''' 4697 SubjectUserName must match an Approved Installer from Section 2. Mismatch → Tier 1 hit.
# '''Pivot ±5 minutes around the install for parent process.''' 4688 of msiexec or installer parent. Suspicious parent → Tier 2 hit.
# '''Check tenant identifier.''' Per-RMM profile defines the extraction (instance hash, AccountId GUID, server URL, etc.). Mismatch → Tier 1 hit.
# '''Check for second RMM on same host.''' Stack 4697 by host across last 30 days. Two distinct RMM families → Tier 1 hit.
# '''Check 60-minute post-install behavior.''' Discovery commands, agent spawning shells, file drops outside install path → Tier 3 hit, upgrade to confirmed compromise.

=== Worked examples ===

==== Example A — Legitimate authorized install ====

Sentinel fires on 4697 for <code>ScreenConnect Client (a3f9c1d2e4b5670a)</code> on WKSTN-1234. Section 2 lookup: ScreenConnect, instance hash <code>a3f9c1d2e4b5670a</code> is the authorized hash. SubjectUserName: <code>NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM</code> (parent: msiexec spawned by ccmexec.exe — SCCM deployment). Install path: <code>C:\Program Files (x86)\ScreenConnect Client (a3f9c1d2e4b5670a)\</code>. Single RMM on host. No discovery commands or agent shell-spawning in the following hour.

'''Disposition:''' Authorized. Close as benign with a one-line note. No tier hits.

==== Example B — Shadow IT (unauthorized but not malicious) ====

4697 for <code>AteraAgent</code> on a marketing department workstation. Section 2 lookup: Atera not on authorized list → Tier 1 hit. Install path: <code>C:\Program Files\ATERA Networks\AteraAgent\</code> (vendor-normal). SubjectUserName: <code>NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM</code>. Parent process (4688 pivot): msiexec spawned by explorer.exe — user double-clicked an installer. AccountId GUID extracted; matches a domain registered to a contractor the marketing team uses. No discovery commands, no agent shell-spawning.

'''Disposition:''' Tier 1 hit on the authorization list, no further hits. Escalate, but workflow is to engage the host owner and the team, not IR. Likely outcome: removal + Section 2 update or formal sanctioning.

==== Example C — Malicious install (confirmed compromise) ====

4697 for AnyDesk service on FILESRV-12. Section 2 lookup: AnyDesk not on authorized list → Tier 1 hit. Install path: <code>C:\Users\Public\Downloads\AnyDesk\AnyDesk.exe</code> → second Tier 1 hit. SubjectUserName: domain user account (not SYSTEM, not approved admin) → third Tier 1 hit. Parent process (4688 pivot): msiexec spawned by powershell.exe with encoded command-line containing <code>IEX (New-Object Net.WebClient).DownloadString</code> → Tier 2 hit. Within 20 minutes of install: <code>whoami /all</code>, <code>net group "domain admins" /domain</code>, <code>nltest /dclist all</code> → Tier 3 hit. AnyDesk listening on TCP 7070 → second Tier 3 hit.

'''Disposition:''' Confirmed compromise. Engage IR immediately. Containment priorities: isolate FILESRV-12, identify and disable the user account (likely compromised credentials), preserve memory for AnyDesk peer-ID extraction (connection_trace.txt), capture outbound netflow, hunt for lateral movement from this host.

== Section 4 — Per-RMM Profiles ==

Reference profiles for the RMMs most frequently observed in current intrusions. Each profile contains the data points needed to execute the [[#Section 3 — Disposition Triage|Section 3]] triage logic — install paths, signer, tenant identifier extraction, and behavioral red flags. Add additional profiles as new RMMs are encountered or as threat reporting shifts.

'''Initial coverage:''' ScreenConnect (because it is our authorized RMM and the tenant-identification logic is critical), AnyDesk, Atera, Action1, SimpleHelp. Next-priority additions: MeshCentral, Tactical RMM, Remote Utilities (RURat), Splashtop, NinjaOne. Tunneling adjacencies (cloudflared, ngrok, RustDesk) tracked separately but follow the same profile structure.

=== ScreenConnect (ConnectWise Control) ===

{| class="wikitable" style="width:100%;"
! style="width:25%; background:#F2F2F2;" | Vendor
| ConnectWise, LLC
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Status in our environment
| '''AUTHORIZED''' — single instance only. Any second instance = unauthorized.
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Threat actor association
|
* Heavily abused both via attacker-owned cloud tenants and via on-prem server compromise.
* '''CVE-2024-1709''' (Feb 2024) — authentication bypass on on-prem ScreenConnect servers; attackers compromised servers and pushed agents to all downstream clients. Continue to validate any on-prem ScreenConnect server is patched ≥ 23.9.8.
* Observed in: Black Basta, LockBit affiliates, Akira, multiple access brokers.
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Process names
| <code>ScreenConnect.ClientService.exe</code>, <code>ScreenConnect.WindowsClient.exe</code>, <code>ScreenConnect.WindowsBackstageShell.exe</code>
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Expected install path(s)
|
* <code>C:\Program Files (x86)\ScreenConnect Client (<INSTANCE_HASH>)\</code>
* <code>C:\ProgramData\ScreenConnect Client (<INSTANCE_HASH>)\</code> — config and session state
* Anything outside these paths is unauthorized.
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Service name(s) — 4697
|
* Service name format: <code>ScreenConnect Client (<INSTANCE_HASH>)</code>
* <code><INSTANCE_HASH></code> is the 16-character lowercase hex identifier tied to a ScreenConnect tenant.
* Our authorized hash: <code><FILL_IN_INSTANCE_HASH></code>. Any other hash present in 4697 Service_Name = unauthorized tenant.
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Expected signer (publisher CN)
| ConnectWise, LLC (formerly Connectwise, Inc / Elsinore Technologies, Inc on legacy builds)
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Network destinations / tenant ID extraction
|
* Outbound to <code><instance>.screenconnect.com</code> or <code><instance>.connectwise.com</code> on 443/8040/8041 (configurable).
* Tenant identifier appears in: <code>--cxn-factory-proxy-detect <fqdn>:<port>;</code> command line, registry <code>HKLM\SOFTWARE\WOW6432Node\ScreenConnect\<HASH></code>, and the parenthetical in service name.
* '''Triage rule:''' if FQDN is not <code><our instance>.screenconnect.com</code> → Tier 1 hit.
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Persistence mechanisms
|
* Windows service (Auto Start) — installed via MSI as SYSTEM.
* Registry: <code>HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\ScreenConnect Client (<HASH>)</code>.
* No scheduled task in default install.
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Specific behavioral red flags
|
* Service hash differs from authorized.
* Binary executing from <code>\ProgramData\<guid>\</code> or <code>\Users\Public\</code>.
* Agent spawning <code>cmd.exe</code>/<code>powershell.exe</code> with command lines containing base64 or invoke-expression patterns — that is the technician execution channel being used by an operator.
* Multiple ScreenConnect Client services on the same host (test/dev sometimes legitimate; otherwise unauthorized tenant chain).
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Triage queries
|
Stack-rank tenants in environment:
<syntaxhighlight lang="splunk">
`win_security` EventCode=4697 Service_Name="ScreenConnect Client*"
| rex field=Service_Name "\((?<instance_hash>[a-f0-9]+)\)"
| stats dc(dvc) as hosts values(dvc) as device_list min(_time) as first by instance_hash
| convert ctime(first)
| sort hosts
</syntaxhighlight>

Detect non-corporate proxy probe target:
<syntaxhighlight lang="splunk">
`win_security` EventCode=4688 "--cxn-factory-proxy-detect"
| rex field=Process_Command_Line "--cxn-factory-proxy-detect (?<fqdn>[^:]+):(?<dest_port>[^;]+);"
| search NOT fqdn="<OUR_INSTANCE>.screenconnect.com"
| stats values(Account_Name) by dvc fqdn dest_port
</syntaxhighlight>
|}

=== AnyDesk ===

{| class="wikitable" style="width:100%;"
! style="width:25%; background:#F2F2F2;" | Vendor
| AnyDesk Software GmbH (publisher CN: <code>philandro Software GmbH</code> on older signed builds; <code>AnyDesk Software GmbH</code> on current)
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Status in our environment
| '''NOT AUTHORIZED.''' Any presence in environment = investigate.
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Threat actor association
|
* Among the most heavily abused RMMs in current intrusions. Frequently deployed as initial-access persistence via phishing or tech-support social engineering.
* Feb 2024 — AnyDesk's code-signing infrastructure was compromised; for a time window, malicious AnyDesk binaries could carry the legitimate publisher cert. Treat signature presence as necessary but not sufficient.
* Observed in: BlackCat/ALPHV, Akira, Conti-lineage groups, Cl0p, Scattered Spider, multiple access brokers.
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Process names
| <code>AnyDesk.exe</code>, <code>AnyDesk_*.exe</code> (installer variants)
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Expected install path(s)
|
* '''Legit install:''' <code>C:\Program Files (x86)\AnyDesk\</code>
* '''Per-user (portable mode):''' <code>%APPDATA%\AnyDesk\</code> — frequently abused; portable mode does not require admin rights, leaves agent running as user.
* '''Operator-deployed:''' <code>\Users\Public\</code>, <code>\Users\<x>\AppData\Local\Temp\</code>, <code>\Users\<x>\Downloads\</code>, <code>\ProgramData\<random>\</code>.
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Service name(s) — 4697
|
* Service name: <code>AnyDesk</code> (when installed in service mode).
* '''Portable mode does not register a service''' — process will appear in 4688 only, not 4697. Important: portable AnyDesk evades the 4697-driven hunt entirely. Cover with file-creation and process-execution telemetry.
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Expected signer (publisher CN)
| <code>philandro Software GmbH</code> (legacy) or <code>AnyDesk Software GmbH</code> (current). Verify against DeviceFileCertificateInfo IsTrusted=true.
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Network destinations / tenant ID extraction
|
* Outbound to <code>*.net.anydesk.com</code> (boot servers) and <code>relay-*.net.anydesk.com</code> on 443/80/6568.
* Listens on TCP 7070 for direct connections (LAN/direct mode).
* AnyDesk does not have a per-tenant FQDN in the same way ScreenConnect does; the AnyDesk-ID (9-10 digit numeric) identifies the agent, configured in <code>%APPDATA%\AnyDesk\service.conf</code> or <code>%PROGRAMDATA%\AnyDesk\service.conf</code>.
* '''Allowlist correlation:''' pull <code>ad.trace</code> and <code>connection_trace.txt</code> from the AnyDesk install during IR — they log peer IDs and connection timestamps.
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Persistence mechanisms
|
* '''Service mode:''' standard auto-start service.
* '''Portable mode:''' no service, no registry persistence; relies on user re-launch — but operators commonly add an HKCU\Run entry or scheduled task to relaunch.
* Unattended access password: configured in <code>service.conf</code>; presence indicates pre-staged remote access.
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Specific behavioral red flags
|
* Portable-mode execution from user-writable paths.
* Non-default unattended access password set immediately after install.
* <code>service.conf</code> modified to set <code>ad.anynet.alias</code> (custom alias for the agent — operator convenience).
* Connection trace file shows peer IDs that have never connected before, immediately after install.
* Listener on 7070 in an environment where direct AnyDesk connections are not used.
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Triage queries
|
Detect AnyDesk presence (process + file events):
<syntaxhighlight lang="kusto">
DeviceProcessEvents
| where FileName =~ "AnyDesk.exe"
| project Timestamp, DeviceName, FolderPath, ProcessCommandLine,
          InitiatingProcessFileName, AccountName
</syntaxhighlight>

Find AnyDesk config file writes (peer ID enumeration):
<syntaxhighlight lang="kusto">
DeviceFileEvents
| where FileName in~ ("service.conf","system.conf","connection_trace.txt","ad.trace")
| where FolderPath has "AnyDesk"
</syntaxhighlight>
|}

=== Atera ===

{| class="wikitable" style="width:100%;"
! style="width:25%; background:#F2F2F2;" | Vendor
| Atera Networks Ltd
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Status in our environment
| '''NOT AUTHORIZED.''' Any presence = investigate as Tier 1.
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Threat actor association
|
* Currently one of the two most-abused RMMs in incident reporting (along with Action1). Free trial requires only an email address; attackers stand up tenants in minutes.
* MSI installer is small, signed, and runs cleanly from a one-liner — making it the canonical phishing-to-persistence payload.
* Observed in: Black Basta, Akira, Medusa, BianLian, multiple access brokers including those tracked as Storm-1811, Storm-1175.
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Process names
|
* <code>AteraAgent.exe</code> (main agent service)
* <code>AgentPackageRunCommand.exe</code> (executes operator-issued commands — high-fidelity post-exploitation indicator)
* <code>AgentPackageHeartbeat.exe</code>, <code>AgentPackageMonitoring.exe</code>, <code>AgentPackageNetworkDiscovery.exe</code>, <code>AgentPackageSTRemote.exe</code>, <code>AgentPackageWindowsUpdate.exe</code>
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Expected install path(s)
|
* <code>C:\Program Files\ATERA Networks\AteraAgent\</code>
* Operator-deployed installs sometimes drop installer to <code>\Users\<x>\AppData\Local\Temp\</code> before MSI execution — surviving artifact in DeviceFileEvents.
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Service name(s) — 4697
|
* <code>AteraAgent</code> (display name: Atera Agent)
* <code>AteraAgentMonitor</code> (newer versions)
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Expected signer (publisher CN)
| <code>Atera Networks Ltd</code>
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Network destinations / tenant ID extraction
|
* Outbound to <code>agent-api.atera.com</code>, <code>app.atera.com</code>, <code>*.atera.com</code>, <code>agentwebsocket.atera.com</code> on 443.
* '''Tenant/customer identifier:''' AccountId GUID embedded in MSI install command line and in registry <code>HKLM\SOFTWARE\ATERA Networks\AlphaAgent\AccountId</code>.
* '''Triage rule:''' extract AccountId from MSI install command-line in 4688 (parent of 4697). If GUID does not match a known authorized tenant — Tier 1.
* Atera publishes its IP ranges; outbound to those IPs is reliable network-side evidence of presence.
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Persistence mechanisms
|
* Windows service (auto-start), installed by msiexec as SYSTEM.
* Registry: <code>HKLM\SOFTWARE\ATERA Networks\AlphaAgent\</code> contains AccountId, FolderId, IntegratorLogin.
* Multiple AgentPackage* helper EXEs registered as scheduled tasks in some versions.
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Specific behavioral red flags
|
* MSI launched by powershell.exe, mshta.exe, wscript.exe, or any Office process — phishing-to-Atera chain.
* <code>AgentPackageRunCommand.exe</code> spawning interactive shells, encoded PowerShell, or LOLBINs — this is the operator executing commands through the Atera console.
* AccountId GUID in registry does not match authorized tenant.
* Install on a workstation that has no managed software-deployment history in SCCM/Intune.
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Triage queries
|
Detect Atera install via MSI from suspicious parent:
<syntaxhighlight lang="kusto">
DeviceProcessEvents
| where FileName =~ "msiexec.exe"
| where ProcessCommandLine has_any ("AteraAgent","setup.msi") and ProcessCommandLine has "AccountId"
| where InitiatingProcessFileName matches regex @"(?i)(powershell|pwsh|mshta|wscript|cscript|outlook|winword|excel|chrome|msedge|firefox)\.exe"
</syntaxhighlight>

Extract AccountId GUID for tenant comparison:
<syntaxhighlight lang="kusto">
DeviceProcessEvents
| where FileName =~ "msiexec.exe" and ProcessCommandLine has "AteraAgent"
| extend AccountId = extract(@"AccountId=([A-Fa-f0-9\-]{36})", 1, ProcessCommandLine)
| where isnotempty(AccountId)
| summarize by AccountId, DeviceName, Timestamp
</syntaxhighlight>

Operator command execution channel:
<syntaxhighlight lang="kusto">
DeviceProcessEvents
| where InitiatingProcessFileName =~ "AgentPackageRunCommand.exe"
| project Timestamp, DeviceName, FileName, ProcessCommandLine, AccountName
</syntaxhighlight>
|}

=== Action1 ===

{| class="wikitable" style="width:100%;"
! style="width:25%; background:#F2F2F2;" | Vendor
| Action1 Corporation
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Status in our environment
| '''NOT AUTHORIZED.''' Any presence = investigate as Tier 1.
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Threat actor association
|
* Sharp uptick from mid-2024 onward; now a co-leader with Atera in unauthorized RMM deployments. Free tier supports up to 200 endpoints with email-only signup.
* Capabilities include patch management and remote command execution — attractive to operators because it doubles as recon / payload-delivery infrastructure.
* Observed in: Akira, Medusa, BianLian, Cl0p, multiple access brokers. Flagged in CISA and Huntress reporting through 2024-2025.
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Process names
|
* <code>Action1_agent.exe</code> (main service)
* <code>action1_remote.exe</code> (interactive remote session client)
* <code>Action1_update_service.exe</code>
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Expected install path(s)
|
* <code>C:\Program Files (x86)\Action1\</code>
* <code>C:\ProgramData\Action1\</code> — config, logs, deployed payload staging (operationally significant — payloads pushed via Action1 land here first).
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Service name(s) — 4697
|
* <code>Action1 Agent</code>
* <code>Action1 Remote Access Tool</code> (when remote session active)
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Expected signer (publisher CN)
| <code>Action1 Corporation</code>
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Network destinations / tenant ID extraction
|
* Outbound to <code>*.action1.com</code>, <code>app.action1.com</code>, <code>agent.action1.com</code> on 443.
* '''Tenant identifier:''' OrganizationId GUID in MSI install command line and registry <code>HKLM\SOFTWARE\Action1\</code>.
* '''Triage rule:''' any Action1 outbound traffic in environment without a matching authorized OrganizationId = Tier 1.
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Persistence mechanisms
|
* Windows service (auto-start), installed by msiexec.
* Registry: <code>HKLM\SOFTWARE\Action1\</code> holds OrganizationId, AgentId, server URL.
* Update service registers as separate Auto service.
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Specific behavioral red flags
|
* <code>C:\ProgramData\Action1\</code> contains executables or scripts dropped within minutes of agent install — operator using Action1 as payload delivery.
* <code>action1_remote.exe</code> spawning shells or LOLBINs.
* MSI install launched by browser, Office, or LOLBIN parent.
* Multiple OrganizationId values appearing across the estate (only one should be authorized, if any).
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Triage queries
|
Detect any Action1 presence:
<syntaxhighlight lang="splunk">
`win_security` EventCode IN (4688,4697)
| search New_Process_Name="*Action1*" OR Service_File_Name="*Action1*" OR Service_Name="*Action1*"
| stats min(_time) as first count by dvc Service_Name New_Process_Name
</syntaxhighlight>

Extract OrganizationId for tenant comparison:
<syntaxhighlight lang="kusto">
DeviceProcessEvents
| where FileName =~ "msiexec.exe" and ProcessCommandLine has "Action1"
| extend OrgId = extract(@"(?i)OrganizationId=([A-Fa-f0-9\-]{36})", 1, ProcessCommandLine)
| where isnotempty(OrgId)
| summarize by OrgId, DeviceName, Timestamp
</syntaxhighlight>

Payload staging in ProgramData:
<syntaxhighlight lang="kusto">
DeviceFileEvents
| where FolderPath has @"\ProgramData\Action1\"
| where FileName endswith ".exe" or FileName endswith ".ps1"
   or FileName endswith ".bat" or FileName endswith ".vbs"
| where InitiatingProcessFileName has "Action1"
</syntaxhighlight>
|}

=== SimpleHelp ===

{| class="wikitable" style="width:100%;"
! style="width:25%; background:#F2F2F2;" | Vendor
| SimpleHelp Ltd
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Status in our environment
| '''NOT AUTHORIZED.''' Special caution: server-side vulnerabilities in scope through 2025.
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Threat actor association
|
* Deployment frequency increased sharply after '''CVE-2024-57727 / 57728 / 57726''' (Jan 2025) — multiple unauthenticated vulnerabilities in SimpleHelp server allowing path traversal, file upload, and remote code execution. Attackers compromised SimpleHelp servers and used them to push remote sessions to managed endpoints.
* DragonForce ransomware adopted the SimpleHelp chain immediately upon disclosure. Multiple other groups followed.
* If any SimpleHelp server exists in environment, server version matters more than client behavior — verify ≥ 5.5.8.
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Process names
|
* <code>SimpleHelpRemoteSupport.exe</code>
* <code>JWrapper-Remote Access.exe</code> / <code>JWrapper-Remote Support.exe</code> (Java wrapper variants)
* <code>remote_access_service.exe</code> (newer naming)
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Expected install path(s)
|
* <code>C:\Program Files\SimpleHelp\</code> or <code>C:\Program Files\JWrapper-Remote Access\</code>
* <code>C:\Users\<x>\AppData\Local\JWrapper-Remote Access\</code> — per-user variant
* <code>C:\ProgramData\JWrapper-Remote Access\</code> — service config
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Service name(s) — 4697
|
* <code>Remote Access by JWrapper</code> or <code>SimpleHelp Remote Access</code>
* Service file path will reference JWrapper-Remote Access directory.
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Expected signer (publisher CN)
| <code>SimpleHelp Ltd</code> or <code>JWrapper Limited</code> (Java wrapper layer)
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Network destinations / tenant ID extraction
|
* Outbound to the SimpleHelp server (customer-hosted). Common ports: 443, 8443, sometimes 80.
* '''Tenant identifier:''' server URL is hardcoded in service config (<code>serviceconfig.properties</code> under <code>JWrapper-Remote Access\JWAppsSharedConfig\</code>).
* '''Triage rule:''' extract server URL from config file. If URL is not a known authorized SimpleHelp server, Tier 1.
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Persistence mechanisms
|
* Windows service (auto-start).
* Java-based wrapper means the running process is often <code>javaw.exe</code> with SimpleHelp jars — process-name hunts must include javaw.exe loading SimpleHelp jars.
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Specific behavioral red flags
|
* '''ANY SimpleHelp presence''' in environments without an authorized SimpleHelp server.
* Connection to a SimpleHelp server FQDN that is not the authorized one.
* Multiple file writes to JWrapper config directory immediately after install — operator pre-staging.
* Unsigned or mismatched-signer jar files in the SimpleHelp directory.
|-
! style="background:#F2F2F2;" | Triage queries
|
Detect SimpleHelp service or process presence:
<syntaxhighlight lang="kusto">
DeviceProcessEvents
| where FileName matches regex @"(?i)(SimpleHelpRemoteSupport|JWrapper-Remote.*|remote_access_service)\.exe"
   or (FileName =~ "javaw.exe" and ProcessCommandLine has "JWrapper")
</syntaxhighlight>

Extract server URL from config (file event):
<syntaxhighlight lang="kusto">
DeviceFileEvents
| where FileName =~ "serviceconfig.properties" and FolderPath has "JWrapper"
| project Timestamp, DeviceName, FolderPath, InitiatingProcessFileName
</syntaxhighlight>
|}

[[Category:Threat Hunting]]
[[Category:RMM]]
[[Category:Detection Engineering]]
